questions.q: Compute the covolume of the lattice $\Lambda = \mathbb{Z}v_1 + \mathbb{Z}v_2$ with $v_1=(2,0)$ and $v_2=(1,3)$ (i.e. $|\det[v_1\,v_2]|$).
questions.hint: Stack the basis vectors as columns of a $2\times 2$ matrix: $\det\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&3\end{pmatrix} = 2\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 0 = 6$.
questions.explain: Stack the basis vectors as columns of a $2\times 2$ matrix: $\det\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&3\end{pmatrix} = 2\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 0 = 6$. The covolume is the absolute value, $6$. Geometrically this is the area of the fundamental parallelogram, and it is independent of the chosen basis — any other basis of the same $\Lambda$ differs by a matrix of determinant $\pm 1$.

questions.q: Two ordered bases of the same lattice $\Lambda$ are $(v_1,v_2)$ and $(v_1',v_2') = (v_1+v_2,\, v_1+2v_2)$. Which matrix $M \in \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ realizes the change of basis $(v_1',v_2') = (v_1,v_2)\,M$?
questions.hint: Reading off coefficients: $v_1' = 1\cdot v_1 + 1\cdot v_2$ gives the first column $(1,1)^T$; $v_2' = 1\cdot v_1 + 2\cdot v_2$ gives the second column $(1,2)^T$.
questions.explain: Reading off coefficients: $v_1' = 1\cdot v_1 + 1\cdot v_2$ gives the first column $(1,1)^T$; $v_2' = 1\cdot v_1 + 2\cdot v_2$ gives the second column $(1,2)^T$. So $M=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&2\end{pmatrix}$, and $\det M = 1\cdot 2 - 1\cdot 1 = 1$, confirming $M\in\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$.

questions.q: Which of the following pairs is a valid $\mathbb{Z}$-basis for the standard lattice $\mathbb{Z}^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^2$?
questions.hint: A pair generates $\mathbb{Z}^2$ iff the determinant of the matrix with those columns is $\pm 1$.
questions.explain: A pair generates $\mathbb{Z}^2$ iff the determinant of the matrix with those columns is $\pm 1$. Option A has $\det = 0$ (collinear). Option C has $\det = 4$, so it generates only the index-$4$ sublattice $2\mathbb{Z}\times 2\mathbb{Z}$. Option D has $\det = 0$ (collinear). Option B has $\det\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix} = 2-1 = 1$, so it is a valid basis.

questions.hint: The classical generators are $T:\tau\mapsto\tau+1$ and $S:\tau\mapsto -1/\tau$, satisfying $S^4=I$ and $(ST)^6=I$.
questions.explain: The classical generators are $T:\tau\mapsto\tau+1$ and $S:\tau\mapsto -1/\tau$, satisfying $S^4=I$ and $(ST)^6=I$. Option B uses $T^2$, which only generates the index-$2$ subgroup of even translations together with $S$ (you cannot reach $T$ itself). Option C generates only the abelian subgroup $\{\pm T^n:n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$, which is far from all of $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$. Option D (translations only) misses $S$ entirely.

questions.q: Points in the same $SL_2(\mathbb Z)$ orbit represent:

hard.q: The fundamental domain $\mathcal{F}$ for $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ acting on $\mathfrak{H}$ is $\{\tau:\,|\tau|\ge 1,\,|\Re\tau|\le 1/2\}$. Which statement correctly identifies an orbit-equivalence on the boundary?
choices: $\tau$ and $\tau+1$ are always identified by $T$, but so are $\tau$ and $-\bar\tau$
The left edge $\Re\tau=-1/2$ is identified with the right edge $\Re\tau=1/2$ via $T$, and the arc $|\tau|=1$ is identified with itself via $S$
Every point on $\partial\mathcal{F}$ is its own orbit representative
hard.explain: $T:\tau\mapsto\tau+1$ glues $\Re\tau=-1/2$ to $\Re\tau=1/2$. On the circular arc $|\tau|=1$, the involution $S:\tau\mapsto -1/\tau$ identifies $\tau$ with $-1/\tau$, which also lies on the arc (since $|-1/\tau|=1$). This makes $\mathcal{F}$ topologically a sphere with one puncture at $i\infty$.
$S(\rho)=-1/\rho$. Since $|\rho|=1$ we have $|-1/\rho|=1/|\rho|=1$. Indeed $\rho$ lies on the arc $|\tau|=1$, so $S$ maps it back to the arc.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in showing that every $\tau\in\mathfrak{H}$ is $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$-equivalent to a point in $\mathcal{F}$?
choices: Apply $S$ first to maximize $\Im\tau$, then apply $T^n$ to center $\Re\tau$, repeating until stable
Apply $T^n$ to bring $\Re\tau$ into $[-1/2,1/2]$, then if $|\tau|<1$ apply $S$ to increase $\Im\tau$; repeat — the process terminates because $\Im(S(\tau))=\Im\tau/|\tau|^2>\Im\tau$ when $|\tau|<1$
Apply $S$ repeatedly until $\Im\tau>1$, then center with $T^n$
The reduction works only for $\tau$ with $\Im\tau>1$
hard.hint: Use $T$ to control $\Re\tau$ and $S$ to push $\Im\tau$ upward.
hard.explain: The standard reduction algorithm: (1) translate by $T^n$ to put $\Re\tau\in[-1/2,1/2]$; (2) if $|\tau|<1$, apply $S$ — this strictly increases $\Im\tau$ since $\Im(S\tau)=\Im\tau/|\tau|^2>\Im\tau$; (3) repeat. The imaginary part is bounded above in each $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$-orbit, so the algorithm terminates.

questions.hint: This is the transformation law for $\gamma=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explain: This is the transformation law for $\gamma=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$.

questions.q: "Holomorphic at infinity" is tested using which variable?
questions.hint: One checks regularity of the Fourier expansion in $q$.
questions.explain: One checks regularity of the Fourier expansion in $q$.

questions.q: If $f$ is a cusp form, what is its constant $q^0$ coefficient?
questions.hint: Cusp forms vanish at the cusp.
questions.explain: Cusp forms vanish at the cusp.

hard.q: The space $M_k(SL_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ of weight-$k$ modular forms is zero for $k$ odd and $k<0$. Which additional value of $k$ gives $M_k=0$?
hard.explain: For $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$, the dimension formula gives $\dim M_k=0$ for odd $k$ (since $-I$ acts as $(-1)^k$), and in particular $M_1=0$. The first nonzero spaces are $M_4$ and $M_6$ (each 1-dimensional), spanned by $E_4$ and $E_6$ respectively.

hard.q: Why does applying the weight-$k$ slash operator twice, $f|_{k}\gamma_1|_{k}\gamma_2$, equal $f|_{k}(\gamma_1\gamma_2)$? Which step in the chain is critical?
choices: The cocycle identity $(c_1\gamma_2\tau+d_1)(c_2\tau+d_2)=(c_{12}\tau+d_{12})$ for matrix multiplication $\gamma_1\gamma_2$
Holomorphicity of $f$ ensures commutativity of the operators
The identity follows from $|c\tau+d|=1$ on the fundamental domain
It uses the fact that $\det\gamma=1$ to cancel cross-terms
hard.explain: The slash operator is $(f|_k\gamma)(\tau)=(c\tau+d)^{-k}f(\gamma\tau)$. Composing two slashes yields a factor $(c_1(\gamma_2\tau)+d_1)^{-k}(c_2\tau+d_2)^{-k}$. The cocycle identity for $GL_2$ shows this product equals $(c_{12}\tau+d_{12})^{-k}$ where $\gamma_{12}=\gamma_1\gamma_2$, making the slash action a genuine group action.

hard.q: Using the dimension formula $\dim M_k = \lfloor k/12\rfloor + 1$ for $k\ge 4$ even and $k\not\equiv 2\pmod{12}$ (approximately), estimate $\dim M_{12}$.
hard.explain: $\dim M_{12}=\lfloor 12/12\rfloor+1=1+1=2$. Indeed $M_{12}$ is spanned by $E_{12}$ and $\Delta$, confirming dimension $2$.

questions.q: At level 1, which pair is emphasized as basic generators?
questions.hint: The graded ring is generated by $E_4$ and $E_6$.
questions.explain: The graded ring is generated by $E_4$ and $E_6$.

questions.q: What is the constant term of normalized $E_4(q)$?

questions.hint: They are concrete holomorphic modular forms.
questions.explain: They are concrete holomorphic modular forms.

hard.q: $E_2(\tau)=1-24\sum_{n=1}^\infty\sigma_1(n)q^n$ is NOT a modular form of weight 2 for $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$. Which transformation law does it satisfy instead?
choices: $E_2(\gamma\tau)=(c\tau+d)^2 E_2(\tau)$ exactly, so it is a genuine weight-2 form
$E_2(\gamma\tau)=(c\tau+d)^2 E_2(\tau) - \frac{6ic}{\pi}(c\tau+d)$, showing a non-trivial correction term
$E_2(\gamma\tau)=E_2(\tau)$ for all $\gamma\in SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$, making it weight 0
$E_2$ diverges at the cusp, so no transformation law exists
hard.hint: The defining sum isn't absolutely convergent, so some cocycle term survives.
hard.explain: $E_2$ transforms with the anomalous correction $-(6ic/\pi)(c\tau+d)$. This makes $E_2$ a quasi-modular form. The combination $E_2^*(\tau)=E_2(\tau)-3/(\pi\Im\tau)$ is non-holomorphic but transforms correctly; $E_2$ itself is the holomorphic shadow.

hard.q: The $q$-expansion of $E_4$ begins $1+240q+\cdots$. What is the coefficient of $q$ (i.e., the $n=1$ term)?
hard.explain: The $q^n$ coefficient of normalized $E_4$ is $240\sigma_3(n)$. For $n=1$: $\sigma_3(1)=1$, so the coefficient is $240$.

hard.q: The discriminant form $\Delta=\frac{E_4^3-E_6^2}{1728}$ is a weight-12 cusp form. Which argument correctly shows $\Delta\ne 0$ on $\mathfrak{H}$?
choices: $\Delta$ vanishes wherever $E_4^3=E_6^2$, but there are no such $\tau$ in $\mathfrak{H}$

$\Delta(\tau)=(2\pi)^{12}\eta(\tau)^{24}$ where $\eta$ is the Dedekind eta function, which is nowhere-zero on $\mathfrak{H}$ because $\eta(\tau)^{24}$ equals an absolutely convergent product $q\prod(1-q^n)^{24}$ with no zeros for $|q|<1$
$\Delta$ is a cusp form, so it must vanish on $\mathfrak{H}$ by definition
$\Delta\ne 0$ because its $q$-expansion has positive leading coefficient
hard.explain: The Dedekind eta function $\eta(\tau)=q^{1/24}\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-q^n)$ has no zeros on $\mathfrak{H}$ since the infinite product converges absolutely and each factor $1-q^n\ne 0$ for $|q|<1$. Hence $\Delta=(2\pi)^{12}\eta^{24}\ne 0$ on $\mathfrak{H}$; it only vanishes at the cusp $i\infty$.

questions.q: Why are q-expansions central in arithmetic applications?
choices: They package modular forms into coefficient sequences used by Hecke and L-functions
questions.hint: Arithmetic information is encoded in Fourier coefficients.
questions.explain: Arithmetic information is encoded in Fourier coefficients.

hard.q: The $q$-expansion principle says a modular form is determined by its $q$-expansion. Which statement is the correct precise version?
choices: Two modular forms of the same weight are equal if and only if their $q$-expansions agree in the first $\lfloor k/12\rfloor+1$ coefficients

A modular form $f$ of weight $k$ for $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is identically zero if and only if its $q$-expansion $\sum a_n q^n$ is the zero power series, because the map $f\mapsto\sum a_n q^n$ is injective
The $q$-expansion determines $f$ only on the arc $|\tau|=1$
Two forms agree if $a_0=b_0$ (constant terms match)
hard.explain: The $q$-expansion $f(\tau)\mapsto\sum_{n\ge 0}a_n q^n$ is injective on $M_k$: a holomorphic modular form that vanishes as $q\to 0$ (i.e., $a_n=0$ for all $n$) must be identically zero, since vanishing to infinite order at $i\infty$ and holomorphicity on $\mathfrak{H}$ force $f\equiv 0$ by the identity principle.

hard.q: The Ramanujan tau function $\tau(n)$ gives the $q$-expansion $\Delta=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\tau(n)q^n$. What is $\tau(1)$?
A newform $f=\sum a_n q^n\in S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ satisfies $a_{mn}=a_m a_n$ when $\gcd(m,n)=1$. Which property of Hecke operators explains this multiplicativity?
choices: Hecke operators $T_m$ and $T_n$ commute and $T_{mn}=T_m T_n$ for $\gcd(m,n)=1$, so their common eigenvalue satisfies $a_{mn}=a_m a_n$
The $q$-expansion is periodic, so coefficients repeat multiplicatively
Multiplicativity follows from the cusp-form vanishing condition $a_0=0$
All weight-$k$ forms have multiplicative coefficients by definition
hard.hint: The $a_n$ are eigenvalues of $T_n$ acting on a common eigenvector.
hard.explain: For $\gcd(m,n)=1$ the Hecke operators satisfy $T_m T_n=T_{mn}$. A newform is a simultaneous eigenfunction: $T_n f=a_n f$. Applying $T_m T_n=T_{mn}$ to $f$ gives $a_m a_n f=a_{mn}f$, so $a_{mn}=a_m a_n$. This is the source of the Euler product for the associated $L$-function.

questions.q: The Petersson inner product on $S_k$ is defined by which integrand against $dx\,dy$?
questions.hint: The factor $y^{k-2}$ combines the weight-$k$ transformation with the hyperbolic measure $y^{-2}dx\,dy$ so the whole thing is $\Gamma$-invariant.
questions.explain: The factor $y^{k-2}$ combines the weight-$k$ transformation with the hyperbolic measure $y^{-2}dx\,dy$ so the whole thing is $\Gamma$-invariant.

questions.q: Why does $\langle f,g\rangle$ converge for cusp forms but fail for general modular forms?
choices: Cusp forms decay exponentially at $i\infty$, beating the growth of $y^{k-2}$
The measure $y^{-2}dx\,dy$ vanishes at the cusp
questions.hint: Near $i\infty$, $|f|\ll e^{-2\pi y}$, so $|f|^2 y^{k-2}$ is integrable; Eisenstein series lack this decay and the integral diverges.
questions.explain: Near $i\infty$, $|f|\ll e^{-2\pi y}$, so $|f|^2 y^{k-2}$ is integrable; Eisenstein series lack this decay and the integral diverges.

questions.q: If $f,g$ are two distinct normalized Hecke eigenforms in $S_k$ with different eigenvalue systems, what is $\langle f,g\rangle$?
questions.hint: Hecke operators are self-adjoint for the Petersson product, so eigenspaces for distinct eigenvalue systems are orthogonal.
questions.explain: Hecke operators are self-adjoint for the Petersson product, so eigenspaces for distinct eigenvalue systems are orthogonal.

hard.q: The Petersson inner product integrates over a fundamental domain $\mathcal{F}$. Which argument shows the integral is independent of the choice of fundamental domain?
choices: The integrand $f(\tau)\overline{g(\tau)}y^{k-2}\,dx\,dy$ is $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$-invariant, so integrating over any two fundamental domains gives the same value
The integral depends on the domain but different choices give values differing by an integer
One must always use the standard fundamental domain $\{|\tau|\ge 1,\,|\Re\tau|\le 1/2\}$
hard.explain: Under $\tau\mapsto\gamma\tau$, the hyperbolic measure transforms as $y^{-2}dx\,dy\to y^{-2}dx\,dy$ (it is $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$-invariant), and $f(\gamma\tau)\overline{g(\gamma\tau)}y^k$ picks up $(|c\tau+d|^{-2k})(|c\tau+d|^{2k})=1$. So the integrand is $\Gamma$-invariant, and any two fundamental domains tile $\mathfrak{H}$ the same way, giving identical integrals.

hard.q: The Petersson norm $\lVert f\rVert^2=\langle f,f\rangle$ satisfies $\lVert f\rVert^2>0$ for $f\ne 0$. Which step in the proof is key?
choices: $|f(\tau)|^2 y^{k-2}\ge 0$ everywhere with equality only if $f(\tau)=0$ pointwise, and a holomorphic function vanishing on an open set is identically zero
Cusp forms have real Fourier coefficients, so $\langle f,f\rangle=\sum|a_n|^2>0$
The hyperbolic metric gives $\mathcal{F}$ finite area, ensuring positivity
Positivity follows from the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality
hard.explain: The integrand $|f(\tau)|^2 y^{k-2}$ is non-negative and equals zero only where $f=0$. If $f\not\equiv 0$, there is an open set where $|f|>0$ (by the identity principle for holomorphic functions). That set has positive hyperbolic measure, so the integral is strictly positive.

hard.q: The space $S_{12}(SL_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ is 1-dimensional, spanned by the normalized newform $\Delta$. If $\langle\Delta,\Delta\rangle=C$ for some constant $C>0$, and $f=3\Delta$, what is $\langle f,f\rangle/C$?
hard.explain: $\langle 3\Delta,3\Delta\rangle=9\langle\Delta,\Delta\rangle=9C$, so $\langle f,f\rangle/C=9$. The Petersson product is sesquilinear, so scaling by $3$ multiplies the norm-squared by $|3|^2=9$.
